speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want a picture of my brother and David at the bar mitzvah with Barbara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] my son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one do you want?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any one. This is OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, there you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] over there, because I'm in position.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Careful that wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Be careful. Just put it down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put it after the-- for the bar mitzvah area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK. Be careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Betty-- Remember you have to be outside of the room during the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so let me get my--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So get whatever you need to get. You can listen. Wherever you're more comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 6, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Manya, M-A-N-Y-A, Winshell, W-I-N-S-H-E-L-L. My name is Joan, J-O-A-N, Benbasat, B-E-N-B-A-S-A-T. This interview is taking place in Lauderhill-- in the city of Lauderhill in the state of Florida, in the United States of America. This interview is being given in English. Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Manya Winshell, M-A-N-Y-A, Manya, Winshell, W-I-N-S-H-E-L-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth? And spell it please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shneider, S-H-N-- N-E-I-D-E-R-.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your first name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manya.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 12, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: So today you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: 78 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city were you born? And could you please spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuczyn, T-U-C-Z-Y-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abram.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And his last name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shneider

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a merchant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember an interaction between you and he--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --something that you've had with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special that I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only to the shul temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the synagogue, OK

speaker: interviewee
text: Synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basia, B-A-S-I-A, and Shneider.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: House wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your relationship with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. I loved her very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it. Tell me about your relationship with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was wonderful. She-- she taught us to sing. She was very good singer. And she was wonderful, a good mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember back to any specific story you can tell about you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No special stories. She used to tell me how to dress, what time to come home, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: She did not. Who else made up the members of your household, Mrs. Winshell?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister Golda.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how old-- when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1918. I don't know exactly the date. And a brother Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: His birthday, do you remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't remember the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my youngest sister [? Lea. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was she born?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with your brother and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like any other brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we were happy. Sometimes we fought, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: What made us happy? When we had everything, made us happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like holidays, when my mother prepared everything for the holidays, especially Passover. We were looking forward for the Passover dishes to take down from the attic. That was the happy times for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your grandparents, did they live in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember my grandparents, because my-- my mother's parents died when I-- my mother's mother died before I was born. And then my grandfather died, I was a baby, maybe two years old. So I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's-- my grandmother I don't remember at all. But my grandfather used to come and stay with us in Tuczyn from his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some interaction with him in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing special.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, children don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Childhood was very nice. In a small town you had a lot of the friends. And at that time, all friend were good if their Jewish or not. We had a good time. And then when I got a little older, I joined Zionist organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were very happy. We used to have a lot of friends in the [NON-ENGLISH]. I belonged to [NON-ENGLISH]. That's all Zionist organizations. We were very happy that time until-- What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school. That's the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: --elemental school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was number one and number two. I belonged to the number one. And then we went to higher education. We had to go to Rowne. That's a city, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a bigger city. And there I went to a gymnasium, which is called [NON-ENGLISH], Polish and Jewish, a mixture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to travel to the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had to stay there. I had to move to that city and stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't travel. It was about 25 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was some of your experiences there? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was normal. It was OK. Before the war everything was OK until the Nazis started.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did any of your schools have-- were religious affiliated? Were they religious affiliations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think conservative. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe you would call it orthodox. But I wasn't very religious, because we belonged to the Zionist organizations. And that was, you know, like [INAUDIBLE] people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like every Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about it. Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which holidays?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of the Jewish holidays. What was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why was that your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of all the Passover dishes and the things my mother used to make. It was eight days. It was nice. It was good holidays. We were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did a lot of things with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother, father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the other holidays? How did you celebrate? You go to synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Rosh Hashana, Yom Kippur, [NON-ENGLISH] was a happy holiday, because we all went to shul with my father. And they had-- you know, they were working with the Torah. And the children were there and kissing the Torah. It was happy days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brother's bar-- was your brother bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. But they didn't make bar mitzvahs like they make in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just went to shul, had the kiddish. He said the Torah, and that's it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like Yiddish, you know, that's all it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said before-- you were talking about you did have to board at your high school. You did have to be boarded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like being separated from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't so far away. My father used to come once and awhile to visit me. And for holidays we went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went home during the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What were some of your hobbies, your interests as a young girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a young girl? First, we were very busy in the Zionist organizations, used to have picnics and things and everything. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I was reading a lot. I liked to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like to read in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. I used to read a lot of books when I was very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sports we didn't have much. I played volleyball. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember is your happiest time when you were a child, your happiest time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being with my family, holidays, going on picnics, that's what we did. That was the happiest times.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first experience anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in school yet. So some of the children-- first of all, it was very unhappy when they said the prayers. We had schools with the Christian children and Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got up and started to cross themselves and say the prayers. And the Jewish children were sitting like dummies. And after we finished-- they finished, they used to say to us, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means you killed our God. What did you feel when that was said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, we felt very bad. But that's the only time that we really experienced the anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 11, 12, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's when it happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It may be even younger, 10, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about other experiences with anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. Later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about as you got older-- as you got older?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got older, it started to come to Poland, the stories from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What stores did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't hear, but they did, the Pollocks. So they started to do the same things.

speaker: interviewer
text: They heard stories--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the Germans about--

speaker: interviewee
text: How to behave with Jews. So they did the same thing. They started to-- to do things to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an example of some of the things that they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know that when the shoichet has to kill the-- the cows, you know, for meat, the kosher. And all of a sudden, in Poland, you weren't allowed to do that anymore. So you couldn't have any kosher meat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember I was already 20 or 21 at that time. And they wouldn't let the shoichet couldn't do that. So the Jewish people couldn't have the meat, only underground they did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did that happen? How did that occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody should know, they went to one place to-- had it. And they divided the-- the meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said the shoichet The shoichet is a rabbi--

speaker: interviewee
text: A shoichet.

speaker: interviewer
text: A shoichet. That is a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is a specially-- how I would I say? He can only kill the cow, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know how spell that? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoichet, S-H-O-I-C-H-E-T, shoichet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What were your direct experiences with anti-Semitism as you were getting older, besides the shoichet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had friends that were Christians. And when they started to hear what's going on there in Germany, they weren't my friends anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did that come about? How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to talk to us. You know, they were bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did feel at that time? What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad, but we couldn't help it. That's it until 1939. It was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did your life change once this news that came from Germany hit the Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish people, I couldn't recognize them. They were different. Then the anti-Semitism showed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say you couldn't recognize them, what you mean by that? You could--

speaker: interviewee
text: They changed. I mean, the-- the people weren't the same people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about at home concerning this.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all upset. But we couldn't do anything about it, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the Yiddish neighbors?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoever surrounded you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of Christian neighbors, which weren't the same. They started to show their true colors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, when you were growing up and you were going to school, what did you hear was happening in Germany? What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't in school anymore when it started to happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was different. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because that started in '38, '37, '38. I was married already.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I was going to ask what was your occupation before the war? What did you do after you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a tutor to children, you know. And later, I had a store. I opened a store when I was 19 years old, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] delicacies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which-- that's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of delicacies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Delicacies-- it's not delicatessen-- delicacies, sardines, all kinds imported stuff, and cheeses, and oranges. It was a small town, so oranges were the big deal, and all kinds of stuff-- even bananas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you had this store?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19 When they opened the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you open the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband did. We weren't married yet, but they helped me, and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 15 years old--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Zionist organizations, two children grew up in the same town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remembering back at the time that you first saw him, what were your feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I fell in love. [LAUGHS] And he did, too. So 15, we were married. I was already 20, 19 and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: June 19th.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: His full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob Winshell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when you were first married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tuczyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: His occupation-- he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a tin smith. And he opened a place that he made all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: For an example--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tin smith.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what?

speaker: interviewee
text: He made things for-- [NON-ENGLISH] I don't really know how to explain it. And he had like a company. And they covered roofs with tin, even churches, all kinds of things. He had a company.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your life together at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got married?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. We had a good life. We were married 56 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When things start to change for you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean during the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, the Germans and the Soviet Union divided Poland. So our part of Poland became Soviet Union, Ukraine. At that time it was Soviet Union. So we were lucky. So we-- we could leave for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let me understand-- when you were a child you've lived in-- the country was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a child it was Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1918?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before 1918, it was Russian. I was born a Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: '18 became Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So at that time, was Poland until '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Going back to what I had just asked you, how did life change in the years 1937, and '38, right prior to the war? How did your life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really much, it was the same as really before. There was anti-Semitism, and that's all. And we were used to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the quality of your life, you have everything that you needed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At that time we had-- yes, until '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did you realize that you had to leave?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened prior to 1941? What events?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39 The Soviet Union came. So they weren't threatened, nothing. Just we had obey what they wanted us to do, that's all. You couldn't have a store. I didn't have a store. I closed the store right away. When they came in, we closed it, nothing. And in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that when you closed the store? What year? 1939. What did you do after you closed the store? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, my husband went to work for the Soviet Union, you know, whatever they gave him work, and he worked. And I was with my baby. I had my baby already. No, I didn't. '40 he was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you have to close your store, Mrs. Winshell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in Soviet Union you can't have business. They would send you to Siberia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We couldn't. If you are smart enough and closed it, you're clean. Otherwise, they would send you to Siberia. Because you can't have business in the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life at home change once this occurred.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we had to stand in line for bread. You couldn't get anything--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1939. But we could still survive. There were no Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we get-- we still knew people that had the-- the food like flour. So we traded. We had our things we traded to them. But the most important thing, if you had to get something, you had to stay in line. There were a few stores, which were government stores, the Soviet Union stores. And we stayed in line and got it. If you get it, you get it. If not, it's too bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Soviets to the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. Nothing wrong. They didn't do anything wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear about the Germans at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Because at that time, you wouldn't hear anything. In the Soviet Union you don't hear anything. [LAUGHS] They don't talk and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so we couldn't hear anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your lifestyle did change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, it changed a lot. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But we were happy that they were there, that we had the Soviet Union. At least our lives weren't threatened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did not hear what was happening elsewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know anything about Germany? No, not while the Soviet Union was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody in your community--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your husband, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was there any discouragement or unhappiness with the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean about the Soviet Union? Nobody could help anything. What would you do? Sure, a lot of people were unhappy. But you couldn't help it. Nobody could do anything. Because if they did something, they went to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of any people that did go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't we stop right there.
